title: Peter the Piccaninny
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He has a name which canן¿½t be brought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Within the sphere of metre;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, as heן¿½s Peter by report,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll trot him out as Peter.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I call him mine; but donן¿½t suppose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Iן¿½m his dad, O reader!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My wife has got a Norman nose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She reads the tales of Ouida.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I never loved a nigger belle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My tastes are too aesthetic!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The perfume from a gin is well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A rather strong emetic.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, seeing that my theme is Pete,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This verse will be the neater
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I keep on the proper beat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And stick throughout to Peter.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We picked him up the Lord knows where!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At noon we came across him
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Asleep beside a hunk of bear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His paunch was bulged with ן¿½possum.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Last stanza will not bear, I own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A pressure analytic;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But bard whose weight is fourteen stone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is apt to thump the critic.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We asked the kid to give his name:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He didnן¿½t seem too willing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The darkey played the darkeyן¿½s game
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We tipped him with a shilling!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We tipped him with a shining bob
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No Tommy Dodd, believe us.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We didnן¿½t ן¿½tumbleן¿½ to his job
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, why did Pete deceive us!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I, being, as Iן¿½ve said, a bard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Resolved at once to foster
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This mite whose length was just a yard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This portable impostor!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This babeן¿½ I spoke in Wordsworthן¿½s tone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (See Wordsworthן¿½s ן¿½Lucyן¿½, neighbour)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll make a darling of my own;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And heן¿½ll repay my labour.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Heן¿½ll grow as gentle as a fawn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As quiet as the blossoms
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That beautify a land of lawn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll eat no more opossums.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The child I to myself will take
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a paternal manner;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ah! he will not swallow snake
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In future, or ן¿½goannaן¿½.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Will you reside with me, my dear?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I asked in accents mellow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The nigger grinned from ear to ear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And said, ן¿½All right, old fellow!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so my Pete was taken home
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My pretty piccaninny!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, not to speak of soap or comb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His cleansing cost a guinea.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But hang expenses!ן¿½ I exclaimed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll give him education:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A ן¿½nigן¿½ is better when heן¿½s tamed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perhaps, than a Caucasian.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ethnologists are in the wrong
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ About our sable brothers;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I intend to stop the song
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Pickering and others.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alas, I didnן¿½t do it though!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Pickeringן¿½s conclusions
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were to the point, as issues show,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mine were mere delusions.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My inky pet was clothed and fed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For months exceeding forty;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But to the end, it must be said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His ways were very naughty.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When told about the Land of Morn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Above this world of Mammon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d shout, with an emphatic scorn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ah, gammon, gammon, gammon!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He never lingered, like the bard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To sniff at rose expanding.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Me like,ן¿½ he said, ן¿½em cattle-yard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fine smell de smell of branding!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The soul of man, I tried to show,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Went up beyond our vision.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You ebber see dat fellow go?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He asked in sheer derision.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In short, it soon occurred to me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This kid of six or seven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who wouldnן¿½t learn his A B C,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was hardly ripe for heaven.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He never lost his appetite
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He bigger grew, and bigger;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And proved, with every inch of height,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A nigger is a nigger.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, looking from this moment back,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have a strong persuasion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That, after all, a finished black
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is not the ן¿½cleanן¿½ Caucasian.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dear Peter from my threshold went,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One morning in the body:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He ן¿½droppedן¿½ me, to oblige a gent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A gent with spear and waddy!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He shelved me for a boomerang
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We never had a quarrel;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, if a moral here doth hang,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why let it hang the moral!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My mournful tale its course has run
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My Pete, when last I spied him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was eating ן¿½possum underdone:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He had his gin beside him.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Kendall represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Kendall's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
